coredata.dc:title: Environmentally degradable carbon dots for inhibiting P. globosa growth and reducing hemolytic toxin

coredata.dc:description: Red tides not only destroy marine ecosystems but also pose a great threat to human health. The traditional anti-red tide materials are difficult to degrade effectively in the natural environment and there may be risks of environmental leakage and secondary pollution. Furthermore, they cannot reduce the toxicity of toxins released by algae. It is very important to prepare degradable materials that can effectively control red tide and reduce their toxins in the future. Herein, degradable CDs (De-CDs) with biocompatibility and non-toxicity is successfully prepared using the one-step electrolytic method. De-CDs can effectively inhibit P. globosa (algae associated with red tide) growth. More importantly, the De-CDs not only can attenuate the toxicity of toxins released by P. globosa, but also can be degraded under visible-light irradiation in the seawater and avoids environmental leakage. The successful preparation of De-CDs provides a new idea for degradable materials with anti-red tide algae in the future.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Degradation
Visible light
Red tide algae
Anti-algal activity

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic preparation process of De-CDs. (b) TEM image of De-CDs. (c) FTIR spectrum of De-CDs. (d) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of De-CDs. (e) Excitation–emission color map of De-CDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) TEM image of the De-CDs before and after 6 days photodegradation (Inset: digital photo of De-CDs solution before and after photodegradation). (b) The size distribution of De-CDs before and after 6 days photodegradation. (c) UV–Vis absorption spectra of De-CDs photodegradation from 0 to 6 days. (d) Amount of CO and CO2 evolution of De-CDs after photodegradation. (e) Amount of CO and CO2 evolution of De-CDs after photodegradation in different atmospheres (air, N2 and O2). (f) Amount of CO and CO2 evolution of De-CDs after photodegradation for different temperatures (4, 25 and 37 °C). (g) Amount of CO and CO2 evolution of CDs after photodegradation in different solvents (Water, seawater, and F/2 medium) (h) Amount of CO and CO2 evolution of CDs after photodegradation under different pH (1–14).

(a) TEMP spin-trapping ESR spectra of De-CDs for TEMP-1O2. (b) DMPO spin-trapping ESR spectra of De-CDs for DMPO-·O2 −. (c) DMPO spin-trapping ESR spectra of ENZ-NCDs for DMPO-·OH. (d) Schematic illustration of De-CDs producing ·O2 − and 1O2 under visible light irradiation.

(a) The full XPS spectrum of De-CDs. (b–c) The high-resolution C 1s, and O 1s XPS spectrum of De-CDs. (d) The full XPS spectrum of De-CDs after 3 days visible-light irradiation. (e–f) The high-resolution C 1s and O 1s XPS spectrum of De-CDs after 3 days visible-light irradiation. (g) The full XPS spectrum of De-CDs after 6 days visible-light irradiation. (h–i) The high-resolution C 1s and O 1s XPS spectrum of De-CDs after 6 days visible-light irradiation. (j) The degradable mechanism of De-CDs.

(a) The impact of different concentrations De-CDs on the viability of Hela cells after 48 h co-incubation. (b) The growth curve of P. globos a (0.5 L, 2.0 × 10 6 cells·L−1) after co-incubation with varying concentrations of De-CDs (0, 50, 100, 200, and 400 μg·mL−1) over 12 days. (c) Typical photographs of P. globosa (The control group: 2.0 × 10 6 cells·L−1) after co-incubation with varying concentrations of De-CDs (0, 50, 100, 200, and 400 μg·mL−1) over 12 days. (d) The hemolytic toxin activity evaluation after treated varying De-CDs concentrations in light and darkness, respectively. The significance of the indicated comparisons was calculated using a two-tailed t-test, *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, and ***p < 0.005. NS indicates no statistically significant difference.

(a)–(c) The CLSM bright field, dark field and merged field images of P. globosa treated with 100 μg·mL−1De-CDs for 30 min at 37 °C. (λex = 405 nm) (d) Algae density (e) Chlorophyll content (f) RuBisCo activity (g) ROS content (h) SOD activity (i) CAT activity of P. globosa treated with and without De-CDs (200 μg·mL−1) for 4 Days. (j–k) The SEM images of P. globosa treated without and with 200 μg·mL−1 De-CDs. The significance of the indicated comparisons was calculated using a two-tailed t-test, *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, and ***p < 0.005. NS indicates no statistically significant difference.
Mechanism diagram of De-CDs against P. globosa.

ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: This paper has been recommended for acceptance by Amit Bhatnagar.

head.ce:title: Environmentally degradable carbon dots for inhibiting P. globosa growth and reducing hemolytic toxin

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Red tides not only destroy marine ecosystems but also pose a great threat to human health. The traditional anti-red tide materials are difficult to degrade effectively in the natural environment and there may be risks of environmental leakage and secondary pollution. Furthermore, they cannot reduce the toxicity of toxins released by algae. It is very important to prepare degradable materials that can effectively control red tide and reduce their toxins in the future. Herein, degradable CDs (De-CDs) with biocompatibility and non-toxicity is successfully prepared using the one-step electrolytic method. De-CDs can effectively inhibit P. globosa (algae associated with red tide) growth. More importantly, the De-CDs not only can attenuate the toxicity of toxins released by P. globosa, but also can be degraded under visible-light irradiation in the seawater and avoids environmental leakage. The successful preparation of De-CDs provides a new idea for degradable materials with anti-red tide algae in the future.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Red tide is mainly caused by harmful algae blooms in a short period of time (Trottet et al., 2022). It can inhibit marine organisms, damage the ecological system and endanger human health (Anderson, 2009; He et al., 2021; Lee et al., 2013). In the past 20 years, the number of red tides show a rising trend worldwide (Dai et al., 2023). More importantly, with the intensification of climate change and the destruction of the ecological environment, these factors have promoted the frequent occurrence of red tide (Gao et al., 2019). Therefore, effective prevention and emergency control of red tide has become a hot issue in recent years. At present, a variety of approaches have been applied for red tide control, such as physical (salvage, ultrasound, solar ultraviolet radiation), chemical (algicide, photocatalysis) and biological (microorganisms, plants, animals) methods (Ebenezer et al., 2014; Hu et al., 2022; Lürling and Tolman, 2014; Sugawara et al., 2003; Xiao et al., 2019; Xu et al., 2019; Zhu et al., 2019). However, these methods show some shortcomings and deficiencies. Physical method requires plenty of human and material resources, and can also affect other aquatic organisms (Sengco, 2009). And the process of mechanical fragmentation frequently leads to the liberation of algal toxins, which results in greater secondary contamination than red tide overgrowth (Wang et al., 2023). Chemical methods are costly and cause secondary pollution (Murray et al., 2010). Biological methods may influence the ecosystem due to the introduction of other new species. So, we need to develop new anti-algal materials with lower economic costs and produce no secondary pollution to deal with the prevention of red tide algae and reduce their released toxins.

Recent years, carbon-based materials (CNMs) have play important roles in the development of material science. They are widely applied in drug carriers, antibacterial agents, bio-sensors, energy storage and conversion devices, water purification substrate and metals recovery based on their unique chemical, electrical, optical, thermal and mechanical properties (Eivazzadeh-Keihan et al., 2022; Jaymand et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2023; Ortiz-Medina et al., 2019; Venkateswara Raju et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2018; Xin et al., 2019; Yang et al., 2023). Allied Market Research also reports that the global CNM market would be projected to reach USD 31.6 billion by 2031 (Pikula et al., 2023a, 2023b). As a new rising star of the CNMs’ family, carbon dots (CDs) has attracted more and more scientists' attention in recent years owing to their small size, non-toxicity, abundant low-cost sources and easy large-scale synthesis. Recently, we have reported that CDs as artificial enzymes can effectively inhibit red tide algae growth (Wang et al., 2023). And the CDs are expected to be a candidate for anti-red tide algae materials. However, the as-prepared CDs was difficult to degrade in the environment. And then may lead to environmental leakage and cause environmental problems in a short time. Therefore, it is imperative to develop environmentally degradable CDs to effectively inhibit red tide algae.

In this work, the degradable CDs (De-CDs) was prepared using vitamin C as raw material by an electrolytic method with platinum wire as the electrode and realized macro preparation. The degradable experimental data showed the De-CDs can be photodegraded into CO2, H2O and CO for 25 days. Among them, CO2 and H2O are the main degradation products and CO is tracing amounts. In addition, the De-CDs also exhibits excellent biocompatibility and non-toxicity. The experimental data showed De-CDs can enter the red tide algae (Phaeocystis globosa (P. globosa)) and remove more than 90% of red tide algae (P. globosa) after 4 days when their concentration is 400 μg·mL−1. More importantly, De-CDs can weaken the toxicity of hemolytic toxins released by P. globosa. And the De-CDs is also degraded by visible-light irradiation in the seawater. So, the preparation of De-CDs not only provides new ideas for the preparation of degradable CDs, but also expands their application in the field of anti-algae.

The platinum as the electrode was put into the 100 mL saturated vitamin C solution. The voltage between platinum electrodes was static potentials 30 V and used a direct current power supply. The electrolyte was continuously stirred for three weeks, and the color changed from colorless to dark-orange. Next step, stop the electrolytic reaction. The as-prepared De-CDs solution was put into a 500 Da dialysis bag for dialysis with deionized water to remove excess ascorbic acid. Finally, the De-CDs was obtained.

A series of different concentrations of De-CDs were added into the a 96-well plate containing Hela cell culture medium and cultured in standard medium at 37 °C in 5% CO2 for 24 h. After 48 h, the Hela cell viability was determined by MTT method. Sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS) as lysate treated the Hela cell. After that the absorbance intensity at 570 nm was recorded using a microplate reader.

A series of different concentrations De-CDs were mixed into the F/2 medium containing P. globosa. And they were incubated at 28 °C and irradiated by 5000 lux LED light for 12 h every day. The growth density of P. globosa was determined by measuring their absorption intensity at 680 nm (Jia et al., 2015) The absorption intensity can reflect P. globosa content. Moreover, the hemacytometer plate was used to measure the density of P. globos a (2.0 × 10 6 cells·L−1) (Camacho-Fernández et al., 2018).

The content of chlorophyll (Chlorophyll a and Chlorophyll b) was extracted by hot ethanol extraction method (Fan et al., 2020). The detailed process is as follows: P. globosa suspension was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 5 min. The precipitate was frozen at −20 °C for 12 h. 80 °C ethanol (95%) was rapidly added into them and ultrasonic crushing for 10 min. The solution was stored at 4 °C for 12h. After being centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 5 min, the supernatant was obtained from the suspension. The UV–visible absorption spectrometer recorded the supernatant absorption values at 649 nm (I649), 665 nm (I665), and 750 nm (I750). To calculate the content of Chlorophyll a and Chlorophyll b, we employed the modified Lichtenthaler formula (Wang et al., 2023).
(1)
C h l o r o p h y l l
a = 13.95 ×
(
I 665
−
I 750
)
− 6.88 ×
(
I 649
−
I 750
)
(2)
C h l o r o p h y l l
b = 24.96 ×
(
I 649
−
I 750
)
− 7.32 ×
(
I 665
−
I 750
)

ce:section.ce:section-title: CAT, SOD and rubisco activity determination
ce:section.ce:para: Catalase (CAT) activity, superoxide dismutase (SOD) activity, and ribulose bisphosphate carboxylase oxygenase (Rubisco) activity of P. globosa were determined using assay kits (superoxide dismutase activity assay kit, catalase activity assay kit and Rubisco activity assay kit (Beijing Solarbio Science & Technology Co. Ltd., China)). The extracting process of CAT, SOD and Rubisco from P. globosa was as follows: P. globosa suspension was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 5 min to remove the supernatant. Next, 2 mL of PBS (pH = 7.0, 4 °C) was added to the centrifuge tube containing the algal sample and incubated at −20 °C for 12 h. The algal PBS solution was then repeatedly subjected to freeze-thaw for three cycles. After centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 3 min, the resulting supernatant was used to measure the activity of CAT, SOD, and Rubisco, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The content of ROS in P. globosa determination
ce:section.ce:para: 2,7- dichlorofluorescein diacetate (H2DCFDA) as a detection probe was applied for the content of ROS in vivo (Rodea-Palomares et al., 2012). 2 mL H2DCFDA solution with a concentration of 20 μmol L−1 was added to 2 mL P. globosa solution. The resulting mixture was then incubated at a temperature of 25 °C for a period of 30 min. After being washed three times with PBS (0.01 mol·L−1, pH = 7.0), they were then dispersed in 500 μL of PBS (0.01 mol·L−1, pH = 7.0). The microplate reader recorded the fluorescence signal of the sample at 530 nm, with an excitation wavelength of 500 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Extraction of hemolytic toxin and their activity determination
ce:section.ce:para:
P. globosa solution was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 5 min, and the supernatant was removed. The mixture solution (CH3OH: CHCl3: H2O = 13: 7: 5) was added to the sedimentation. Then the mixture was broken by ultrasonic cell crusher (600 W) at 4 °C for 30 min, and the lower layer was dissolved in methanol after standing, which was the crude extract of hemolysin. 0, 50, 100, 200 and 400 μg·mL−1 De-CDs treaded hemolytic toxin were irradiated with visible light for 12 h. And then 0.5 mL citric acid buffer (pH = 7) mixed with 0.5 mL De-CDs treaded hemolytic toxin, and the mixture and 0.5% (v/v) rabbit red blood cells were diluted using citric acid isotonic solution. Then they were incubated in water bath at 37 °C for 30 min. The mixture was centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 10 min. The upper solutions were recorded their absorbance intensity at 540 nm. The degree of hemolytic activity was calculated using the following formula:
(3)
H e m o l y t i c
a c t i v i t y
( % )
=
(
I s
−
I i
)
/
(
I 0
−
I i
)
× 100 %
where I0: absorbance at 540 nm of the positive control (the fully hemolyzed solution), Ii: the negative control (the isotonic solution) and Is was the experimental group (Chen et al., 2021; Peng et al., 2005; Wang et al., 2023).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The SEM sample preparation of P. globosa
ce:section.ce:para: P. globosa treated with and without De-CDs were centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 5 min, and the supernatant was then removed. 2.5% glutaraldehyde add into the centrifuge tube contain the P. globosa and they soaked for 4 h. After that, P. globosa was soaking and centrifugation using 30%, 50%, 70%, 85%, 95%, and 100% concentrations ethanol. Finally, the P. globosa ethanol solution was dropped onto the silicon wafers and drying, and they were observed using a scanning electron microscope.

The anti-algae, the related physiological indexes of P. globosa and the degradation curve of De-CDs, were conducted in triplicate and these results were presented as mean ± standard deviation (SD). The significance of the differences between the groups was calculated using a two-tailed t-test. Differences were considered statistically significant at P < 0.05. Data were analyzed using Origin 2021 (Origin Lab, USA) and Prism software package (Prism 7.0, USA).

The vitamin C as raw material was prepared De-CDs by electrolytic method (Fig. 1 a). Fig. 1b displayed the TEM image of De-CDs, the data shown good dispersion and its particle size distribution was ∼4.5 nm. As shown in Fig. 1c, there are many typical peaks in FTIR of De-CDs, including O–H stretching vibration (3447 cm−1), CO stretching vibration (the existence of carboxyl groups) (1641 cm−1) and C–O–C stretching vibration (1067 cm−1) (Li et al., 2018). The result indicated that De-CDs had hydroxyl and carbonyl groups on the surface. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of De-CDs shown that De-CDs had π-π * transition absorption peak at 224 nm and n-π * transition absorption peak at 292 nm (Fig. 1d) (Jiang et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2020). The excitation–emission map of the De-CDs revealed it had an excitation-dependent emission property and their optimal excitation and emission occurred at 475 nm and 483 nm, respectively (Fig. 1e).

It is worth noting that the De-CDs can be degraded by the visible-light. The color of De-CDs solution gradually faded with the increase visible light irradiation time. And the TEM images of De-CDs shows their diameter reduces from ∼4.5 nm to ∼2.5 nm under the visible-light irradiation (Fig. 2 a–b). In addition, the characteristic absorption peak intensity at 292 nm of De-CDs also gradually decreases with extending visible-light irradiation time (Fig. 2c). Because the characteristic absorption peak intensity at 292 nm of De-CDs disappeared the De-CDs can be completely degraded when the De-CDs was irradiated by visible-light for 36 days (Fig. S1). Gas Chromatography (GC) was used to further identify the gas products of De-CDs degradation. The experimental data shown that they can be degraded to produce CO2 and CO by the visible-light (Fig. 2d). To investigate the influencing factors of De-CDs photodegradation process. The amount of CO2 and CO were the highest in the air, N2 and O2 atmosphere, the shown that O2 played an important role in their photodegradation process (Fig. 2e). Moreover, we found that the amount of CO2 and CO increased with the rising temperature (Fig. 2f). The experimental data indicated that high temperatures can promote the De-CDs degradation. Fig. 2g–h shows the amount of CO and CO2 of De-CDs photodegradation for different solvents and pH. For the different solvents (Water, seawater, and F/2 medium), the amount of CO and CO2 was higher in seawater and F/2 medium than in Water (Fig. 2g). And it may be due to the accelerated photoelectron transport in seawater and culture medium that promoted the De-CDs photodegradation process. Furthermore, the CO2 and CO signal can be detected when the pH value of the solution was between 1.0 and 11.0. But, the amount of CO2 was lower than CO in pH from 13.0 to 14.0, which was due to the as-produced CO2 reacts with lye to form carbonate (Fig. 2h). Based on the above results, De-CDs can photodegrade to produce CO and CO2 in the air, and the amount of produced CO was too low to cause pollution in the atmosphere.

Next step, the electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) characterizations to further proved the degradable mechanism of De-CDs under visible-light irradiation. Trapping agents such as 5, 5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO) and 2, 2, 6, 6-tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP) were used to confirm the type of ROS. The EPR spectra of De-CDs for 30 min visible-light irradiation shows a 1:1:1 ‧O2 − and 1O2 triplet signal (Fig. 3 a–c) (Li et al., 2023). The experimental data showed that the De-CDs can produce ‧O2 − and 1O2 under visible-light irradiation. And the as-produced ROS by carbon dots was related to electron and energy transfer under light irradiation (Li et al., 2023; Nosaka and Nosaka, 2017). So, the proposed pathways of ‧O2 − and 1O2 formation and transformation was as follows: firstly, De-CDs can be excited to generate 1De-CDs* under visible-light irradiation, and 1De-CDs* rapidly transformed into excited triplet states (3De-CDs*) duo to intersystem crossing, then 3De-CDs* reacted with O2 forming 1O2 via an energy transfer (Chen and Jafvert, 2011; Chen et al., 2019; Qu et al., 2013). In addition, De-CDs will generate the electron−hole pairs (e− and h+) when visible light was irradiating them (Wang et al., 2017). The e−can reduce O2 to form ·O2 −, and some ·O2 − was also oxidized to 1O2 by the (h+). Finally, De-CDs can generate ROS (‧O2 − and 1O2) through electron and energy transfer under the visible-light irradiation (Fig. 3d).

To confirm the degradation process of De-CDs, X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were used to investigate their chemical structure changes. Fig. 4 a–i displayed the XPS data of De-CDs under continuous visible-light irradiation for 0, 3 and 6 days. The XPS spectra of De-CDs indicate that the carbon content increased from 52% to 64%, while the oxygen content decrease from 48% to 36% as the visible-light irradiation time is extended. The result shown that the structure of De-CDs had changed under the visible-light irradiation (Fig. 4a, d and 4g). Moreover, the high-resolution C 1s XPS spectrum of De-CDs shows that the C–C/CC peak area decreases from 63.77% to 45.70%, while the C–O and CO peak area increases (C–O: from 19.17 to 27.89%; CO: from 17.06 to 26.41%), indicating oxidization of C–C/CC into C–O and CO (Fig. 4b, e, 4h and Table S1). In O 1s spectra of De-CDs, the increasing CO and C–OH peak area accompany by the decreasing OC–O peak area (Fig. 4c, f, 4i and Table S2). The decreasing OC–O peak area in O 1s spectra indicating the OC–O may be forming CO2 by as-produced ROS. And the increasing CO and C–OH peak area in O 1s spectra also indicated the C–C/CC were continuously oxidized to form C–O and CO. The trace amount of CO produced may be due to incomplete oxidation of C–C/CC. After reviewing the experimental results mentioned above, the degradable mechanism of De- CDs was as follows: firstly, the De-CDs had the ability to generate ROS (·O2 − and 1O2) when exposed to visible light. Secondly, the as-generated ROS can oxidize C–C/CC and OC–O of De-CDs. Finally, De-CDs was oxidized to CO2, CO and H2O (Fig. 4j).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation and mechanism research of De-CDs anti-algae
ce:section.ce:para: In the following experiments, the cytotoxicity of De-CDs was studied before evaluating their anti-algal activity. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the Hela cells co-incubated with varying concentrations of De-CDs for 48 h. The cell viability reaches 90.62% at 800 mg·mL−1 De-CDs. The results shown De-CDs had low toxicity and good biocompatibility. Next step, P. globosa as red tide model organisms was used to verify the anti-red tide algal of De-CDs. The growth curves of the different concentrations De-CDs treaded P. globos a (0.5 L, 2.0 × 10 6 cells·L−1), and shown that their density decreased with the increasing concentration of De-CDs. The De-CDs (400 μg·mL−1) can completely inhibit the P. globosa growth when P. globosa incubated with De-CDs after 2 days (Fig. 5b and S2). After 12 days, the digital photograph of P. globosa treated with different concentrations of De-CDs revealed that the color of the solution gradually faded with an increase in the concentration of De-CDs. (Fig. 5c). To further demonstrate the accuracy of optimal algal resistance concentration, we expanded the volume of the anti-algae experimental system (10 L, 2.0 × 10 6 cells·L−1), and the results indicate that the De-CDs still showed excellent anti-red tide algal at 400 μg·mL−1 (Fig. S3). In addition, the effect of De-CDs on the growth of P. globosa with different densities showed that the De-CDs (400 μg·mL−1) can effectually inhibit the algal growth and decrease their density in high-density P. globosa (Fig. S4). More importantly, the P. globosa would release the hemolytic toxin after their death (Song et al., 2023). Furthermore, the effect of De-CDs on the toxicity of the hemolytic toxin released by P. globosa was investigated under visible light irradiation, and the data indicated their hemolytic toxicity reduced with the increasing De-CDs concentration under the visible-light irradiation (Fig. 5d). This experimental data implied that De-CDs could reduce the hemolytic toxin activity released by P. globosa under visible-light irradiation. In vivo experiments with P. globosa showed the hemolytic toxin activity of De-CDs treaded P. globosa in light was lower compared with that in darkness (Fig. S5). Since De-CDs can generate ROS (‧O2 − and 1O2) under visible light irradiation, the as-generated ROS may oxidize and destroy the hemolytic toxin cause a reduction in hemolytic toxic activity. To further investigate the toxicity of degraded De-CDs solution, the Hela cells were incubated with De-CDs solutions of different degradation times for 48 h, Fig. S6 shown the toxicity of De-CDs decreased with extending degradation times. So, the De-CDs would not cause secondary pollution to the sea water after inhibiting the growth of P. globos a.

Next step, to further determined the mechanism of De-CDs anti-algae, the distribution of De-CDs in P. globosa, density of P. globosa, chlorophyll content of P. globosa, related enzyme activity of P. globosa and ROS content of P. globosa will be studied. The confocal laser images of De-CDs treaded P. globosa shown the fluorescent signal of De-CDs can be detected and the result indicated De-CDs can permeate into the P. globos a (Fig. 6 a–c) (Y. H. Li et al., 2019). When the De-CDs was incubated with P. globosa for 4 days, the density and chlorophyll content of P. globosa were lower than the control group (without De-CDs) (Fig. 6d and e). Ribulose bisphosphate carboxylase oxygenase (RuBisCO) plays a crucial role in the Calvin cycle as an important enzyme. (H. Y. Li et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2022). So, the RuBisCo activity of P. globosa was studied. As shown in Fig. 6, the De-CDs can reduce the RuBisCo activity of P. globosa which suggested that De-CDs can reduce photosynthesis of P. globosa. Moreover, because the De-CDs can release ROS under the visible-light irradiation, the ROS content was detected in P. globosa. Fig. 6g shows that the ROS content in vivo was significantly increased compared to the control group. These release ROS would lead to oxidative stress and activated the antioxidant system of algal to protects themselves (Halliwell, 2006; Khanna et al., 2023; Pucciariello and Perata, 2021; Ren et al., 2021). To clarify whether the ROS produced by De-CDs will cause the antioxidant system of P. globosa. We detected the SOD and CAT activity in P. globosa, and found that their activity increased compared with the control groups (Fig. 6h and i). The experimental data indicated that De-CDs could start up the antioxidant system of P. globosa. The antioxidant system and membrane can be damaged if the ROS level exceeds the algal capacity to resist, leading to algal death (Aihemaiti et al., 2020; Basit et al., 2022a, 2022b; Wang et al., 2017). Furthermore, the algae density of De-CDs treated P. globosa also decreased obviously (Fig. 6d). So, in order to further determine whether De-CDs would destroy the structure and morphology of algae. The SEM images of P. globosa with and without De-CDs were observed. Fig. 6j and k, the cytoderm of P. globosa was destroyed after being treated with De-CDs, and this could be attributed to the excess ROS produced by De-CDs under visible-light irradiation.

Based on above the results, as shown in Fig. 7 , the mechanism of De-CDs against P. globosa was as follows: firstly, the De-CDs entered the P. globosa and they can generate the ROS (·O2 − and 1O2) under visible-light irradiation. Secondly, the De-CDs can reduce the RuBisCO activity leading to weaken the photosynthesis of P. globosa. Furthermore, as-generated ROS can increase the SOD and CAT activity due to activating the antioxidant system of P. globosa. At the same time, the ROS can reduce the chlorophyll content of P. globosa and further reduces the photosynthesis of P. globosa. Finally, De-CDs lead to P. globosa rupture and death. In addition, De-CDs can also reduce the toxicity of hemolytic toxins released by P. globosa under visible-light irradiation. More importantly, the released De-CDs can also be degraded under visible-light irradiation after P. globosa death.

In summary, degradable De-CDs were macroscale synthesized by a simple electrolysis method using vitamin C as raw material. The De-CDs with biocompatible (400 μg·mL−1) can completely inhibit the P. globosa growth after 2 days. More importantly, they can efficiently reduce the toxicity of hemolytic toxin released by P. globosa. And the De-CDs could be oxidized and degraded into CO2, CO and H2O by as-produced ROS under visible-light irradiation. Therefore, as-prepared De-CDs not only can inhibit the P. globosa growth and reduce the toxicity of toxins released by P. globosa, but also can be degraded by visible light and avoid secondary pollution of the environment. The successful preparation of De-CDs provides a new idea for degradable materials with anti-red tide algae in the future.

Hao Li: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Investigation. Chengming Sun: Supervision, Investigation. Mengling Zhang: Validation, Software, Investigation. Huibo Wang: Investigation, Conceptualization. Yu Chen: Validation, Supervision. Jun Song: Validation, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation (2023A1515011499/2022A1515111176), Shenzhen Science and Technology Program (JCYJ20210324095613036/JCYJ20210324095810028). A Project Funded by the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD), Supported by the Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic Devices and Systems of Ministry of Education and Guangdong Province.
